sentences.text: Correct.
Yeah, because I-- I think this experience shows that why my mind became as it became later in life.
And in school, for instance, we were taught to solve problems and to get challenges.
And although practical, but solvable as an answer, was make the wooden holder so much bigger that it would make up for the heavy steel part, which would take it underwater.
So if you make the wooden part large and long enough, that will hold the axe above water.
That was one of my solutions.
And other solution was that make the whole thing hollow, empty inside.
And then also it would float.
So I have no problem of working with my mind.
Said, ?] I put the paper.
I damped the paper and put it on a-- on a round wooden object on top of it.
And I would glue it down and put oil on top of it.
And when it dried, the paper expanded and became strong like rubber.
And you could play like a drum.
So I suggested this to the teacher, and most of the schools in Hungary used my method and-- so it-- the water wouldn't come in when it was raining and things like that.
And so that's how my mind was working as a kid.
And-- Peter, can you tell us what was your first impression of anti-Semitism in Hungary.
We were going to-- we were going with my father at that-- at a place for a vacation.
And there was a tennis court.
And I was walking close to the tennis court and watch people playing tennis.
And my father was walking around.
And then he came to me, and he looked at my arm.
And he said, oh my god.
And spit was hanging on my coat.
Somebody was spitting at me.
And I saw a big sign that saying, [GERMAN].
It meaning juden, you know, in German, Jewish people are not allowed in this area.
It was-- I think that was around the Balaton Lake in-- in Hungary.
And I was probably-- probably 11 years old or so.
That's-- that's how I-- that's how I met anti-Semitism [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] first.
And it was-- I wasn't so much upset as my father was.
And then I got upset because my father was upset.
That was-- no, no no, that was probably 1941.
And actually, actually, life changed in Hungary slowly.
I would say as Adolf Hitler slowly was getting, slowly in power in Germany around '37 or so, in Hungary, things started changing.
They became what's called the Jewish laws.
Said what Jews can and cannot do.
It was pretty mild at the beginning.
Jews couldn't just go to certain schools to become doctors or attorneys, and stuff like that.
But-- and-- or Jewish people couldn't hold certain businesses and stuff like that.
What we called it Jewish laws.
And us-- we were progressing ahead in time.
It became just more vicious and stronger laws.
And then became physical when they started taking people away into forced labor, forced labor camps, and so on.
Yes.
Yes, after-- I would say in Hungary, it was after 1943 when-- when things-- after the Germans came in in-- in-- March 1943.
Did-- did-- my-- would my father talk to me about the Jewish problems prior to 1943?
No, he wasn't.
The only time-- only time I [? fronted ?] my father in 1943, March 19th, when the Germans moved in.
And I looked at my father, and I looked in his eye.
And I asked him.
I said, Father, is it a possibility now that the Germans going to execute us?
And then he looked at me, and he said yes.
And that was one of the most horrible moments in my life.
When a 13-year-old kid, I cannot describe the feeling that a 13-year-old kid would suddenly feel that he is-- he's sitting in San Quentin's in death row for no particular reason whatsoever.
My father was a law-abiding citizen.
He gave.
He helped the poor and other people.
He helped a lot.
And everybody liked us, and so on.
But it was one of the most horrible feelings in-- in in my life I can recall, that particular statement.
And--
Yes.
Yes, yes.
And then after the Germans moved in, they-- they started moving pretty strong.
And they started bringing in new laws and things as what the Jews can and what the Jews cannot do.
And then after a while, we had to wear a star, the Star of David, a yellow Star of David.
We had to-- everybody had to wear it.
And-- But the Germans did not succeed very easily in Hungary, just like they didn't succeed very easily in Denmark.
Although in Denmark, they had a much harder time than they did in Hungary.

But in Hungary, our-- the-- the president of the country, Horthy, was a pretty respected and strong person who-- for instance, like Raoul Wallenberg from Sweden-- you heard about that person who saved thousands and thousands of Jewish people.
He was similar in a certain sense because the Germans said, OK, Jews have to wear Star of David.
So Horthy gave out papers for Jews for exceptional good work for the society that they did not have to wear the Star of David.
And for instance, our family got one and thousands of others.
You see what I mean?
And so we were able to-- to go on the street with-- with-- it had a special word for it.
I don't remember.
But it was a Horthy paper that you were all right.
You were Jewish.
But you say stop, Jew.
And you ask why you wearing the Star of David?
You had the paper of why not.
And so that worked for a while.
But then as the war-- war was progressing, the next step was to establish the Jewish houses.
It was prior to getting the ghetto set up.
And in a big neighborhood, they would select a home where already, a lot of Jewish people living-- I mean a house.
And then all the Christian people would move out of that house.
And all the Jewish people from the neighborhood would have to move in.
And that's what happened to us.
And so it was called the [? judohaus, ?] meaning just what it is, it's a Jewish house.
And--
[SIGHS] That was not very much after the Germans came into Hungary, but I don't recall exact time as when it was.
Anyway, probably three to six months, or something like that, after the Germans moved in.
Yes.
Yes.
Before we moved to the Jewish house-- oh yes.
Oh yes.
Oh yes.
We had two-- two young kids from Poland who lived with us.
They were refugees from Poland who-- who escaped somehow, Poland, a little boy and a little girl.
Yes, yes.
And that shows the difference, how much difference there was between Hungary and Poland.
The Jewish people were able to-- to give refuge to Jewish people.
And so live-- they lived with us, two very nice-- I remember, in particular, the boy's face.
If I could draw, I could make a painting of him because I-- I remember that little Polish boy.
No.
No.
But don't forget they were-- they were-- they were what you would call not legally.
They were illegal-- illegally in Hungary.
They crossed the border somehow, I don't know how, from Poland.
And I even don't know how they got to us.
I don't know.
I mean, they might have-- there must have been a hell of a lot of different things happening, you know, that they finally arrived in our house.
Actually--
Actually, we had also a villa in a-- in a beautiful mountain, which was on the Buda side of Budapest.
Because Budapest is-- is from the side of Buda and Pest.
That makes the city of Budapest.
And Buda is the side of Budapest where you have all the mountains and the beautiful scenery.
And we had a huge villa there.
So every summer we would be staying in our villa and not in-- in-- in Pest side.
And so that's where the-- that's where the Polish, the two kids-- maybe they were brothers and sisters.
Do you have any [? thoughts ?] from them?
Do you remember them?
I mean, where were you-- were you playing with them, or--?
Yes, I was playing with them.
I was playing with them.
It's-- you have to remember that it's just such a long time-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]
No.
When-- when-- it's interesting.
When-- when you're thinking-- thinking back in such an enormous, huge distance in time, and then also as you grow older, your memory is not so sharp anymore.
So there are several things working against you.
I have certain images of them.
But there is very little I do remember about them, you know.
It's-- it's-- I have no idea was what we would have-- I remember one thing because it's a bad thing I'd done.
That they told me that they gonna have to leave-- and I still don't know why.
I remember this conversation.
They said that next week, Friday, they have to leave-- leave us and go.
And I don't know why, but I lied to them, and I said, Saturday we're gonna have a big children's party here.
And it's a shame you have to leave.
And I remember that.
I remember this conversation.
They left.
What do you mean by-- what-- it was probably 1944 sometimes that they were--
Just maybe a month or so.
They-- they couldn't leave much longer because the only image I have of them was in the villa.
And in the villa, we just stayed during the summer time.
Yes.
Well, we had to leave the-- the apartment house where we lived.
And-- and we had to move into a small-- small place.
And I think-- I think we even didn't get a whole-- whole apartment, but just part of an apartment.
And then, in certain rooms, different people would live, I think.
But I have a vague recollection of that, vague recollection.
Yes, my sister, my father, and my mother, and me.
And my father had connection in-- in-- in the Hungarian ambulance service.
So he thought it'd be a good thing for him to escape the forced labor camps to work as a-- as a-- as a driver for the ambulance service, which he did, which he did.
And so I don't recall him-- I don't recall him being in the Jewish house with us.
I don't-- it's strange enough, but I don't remember now.
Well, what-- what the Germans done when they came in, they froze every Jewish asset.
And whatever he had in banks, and real estate, and everything, that was all what's called frozen, or you can say taken away.
Well, I was sad in this-- in this house.
And I lived there, and I lived there till 1944, October 15th.
Well, when Horthy-- Horthy went to the Hungarian radio station and made a declaration that the Germans are dogs, and they forced us into a war we never wanted to be in.
And the war is over.
The Jews are free, and so on, and so on.
So that was October 15, 1944.
So we threw away our yellow stars, and we were very happy.
And when things were going better, and we had-- we had a maid who worked for us for, I don't know, 15 years or whatever, a very long time.
But she couldn't work for us during the German era.
She worked for another family in Budapest.
She let us-- and that family, because of the fear of the Russians coming into Hungary, left Hungary.
She called us up and said, come on over to our apartment, and you can hide out here.
Yes.
So that's where we went, the whole family.
We went October 15th.
We were all hiding out there.
And-- however, there was a party in Hungary like you have here now, the skinheads, called the Arrow Cross Party in Hungary, which was the skinheads.
You call them Nazis or skinheads.
And a man named Szalasi was the leader, and he took over.
And he was even more-- more sadistic than the Gestapo and the Germans were, and so on.
And so from then on, we had to go into hiding, 100%.
We couldn't stay in that apartment where-- where our maid was staying.
So what happened, we all split up.
My father went back to-- to the ambulance service, where he was driving then an ambulance.
My sister and my mother went up to hide out with nuns.
And I was hiding separately, by myself, in a Catholic school.
And that's where I was hiding for a long time.
And I was extremely bored.
It's stupid, but I'm saying.
But I was bored.
And so I thought, I'm-- I'm not gonna to stay here, I'm going to go out for a walk.
And oh, I could [INAUDIBLE] chances with the Nazis and the Arrow Head people and everything-- not Arrow Head, Arrow Cross, Arrow Cross.
And so I just went out on the street, and I said, I'm gonna go and see a movie.
So I walked about three miles to a movie.
I-- I was familiar with the movie house.
And this is some story, and some interest, but nobody ever can explain what really happened here.
But that's why my sister is alive today.
What happened was that I was walking to this movie house, and-- and the movie house was bombed to the ground.
And I was so shocked, and I said-- that I was just standing there.
And I didn't-- I didn't go anywhere.
Now, he-- his looks were 100% non-Jewish.
And he spoke fluent Serbian and Russian.
He spoke pretty good German.
And actually, he worked for the underground.
But for the underground, he was involved with the Arrow Cross Party.
So that he was a spy for the underground.
So he-- the underground in Hungary was informed as what's the next move of the Nazis, the Hungarian Nazis.
And he asked me, where is your sister and mother?
So I told him.
And he didn't say anything to me.
And then we said goodbye.
But later on I found out that next morning, he went up to this place where my sister was staying.

And what happened was [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] he went up and found my sister and my mother and-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and brought them down from this place where-- where the nuns were because he knew that Arrow Cross people will go out and execute everybody the next day.
And that's how my sister and mother survived that.
